circumstances.circumstancesOfRecovery: On August 2nd, 1969, this unidentified man, who appears to be approximately 18 to 25 years of age and may have Hispanic roots or origins, left from New York City by Napierville Junction RR train no. 9 and arrived in Canada at Montreal, Quebec.  Prior to leaving New York, he had reported to the draft board on Broadway and 52nd and they had directed him to report to Fort Dix, New Jersey.  That is when he decided to flee to Canada.   On August 5th, 1969, he attempted to return to the U.S. when the U.S. Immigration at Champlain, New York State refused him re-entry because he was unable to convince him he was born and raised in New York City.  He claims he was born at home with birth never registered with the state.  Sent back to Canada, he subsequently settled in the Toronto, Ontario, Canada area and has remained since while attempts to verify / establish his true identity and verify his New York City birthplace continue.  We know this subject was present in New York City for many years well documented from at least 1960 to 1969, and claims he was born in Manhattan where he spent his youth. Prior to leaving New York, he claims he spent a lot of time in the area of 3rd Avenue and 47th or 48th Street and may have attended the Greek Orthodox Church on 24th street between 8th and 9th Avenues.  We have very strong reason to believe he had and likely still has family in the U.S. and possibly in the New York City area where he claimed to have lived with his parents and sibling(s) prior to August 1969.  His father may have been a seaman. This Agency is seeking the aid of the public in assisting us to positively establish his true identity and nationality and locate his family members, ideally.

clothingAndAccessoriesArticles.description: Subject arrived with $2.00, a shaving kit, New York City Library Card,  Selective Services Registration Card dated June 24, 1969 in identity of one Alex Pitney, and a U.S.  Social Security Card # 079 46 7451 in this Alex Pitney Identity.

circumstances.circumstancesOfRecovery: Discovered in the woods in a laundry bag off of Lafferty Lane in Dover, DE 19901.

Assistance in this investigation was requested on 03/06/1962, by Sheriff Millis of the New Hanover County Sheriff’s Office. On or about 02/28/1962, or 03/01/1962, two human skulls and a quantity of human bones were discovered by a bulldozer operator in a field being excavated by the Timne Corporation of Wilmington, which is in New Hanover County. This discovery was reported to Sheriff Millis on 03/06/1962. The above bones were taken to Dr. Geoffrey Coe of the Department of Anthropology at the Memorial Hospital of the University of North Carolina for anthropological examination in order to determine the age and other pertinent information concerning these bones. In November of 2021 the remains were taken to Dr Ann Ross at the North Carolina Human Identification and Forensic Analysis Laboratory.

circumstances.circumstancesOfRecovery: An unidentified male was found 10/26/1965 in the southeast corner of an alfalfa field near HWY 36 and HWY W. The land owner reported that the alfalfa field was harvested the last week of July in 1965 or the first week in August of 1965 and the unidentified male was not there at that time. The unidentified male was found lying in the push up position. Men's gray twill work pants with bright blue paint on them were found a few feet away from the unidentified male, pockets were empty. A black leather belt, with a missing buckle, were found on the pants. Shoes were not found. A blue nylon jacket was found approximately 1000 feet away from the unidentified male. The unidentified male was wearing a white sweatshirt with red trim at the wrist and waist band.
